title: Locally resolved electronic textures of reconstruction domains in marginally twisted monolayer-bilayer graphene
authors: Sean M. Walker, Patrick Sarsfield, Isaac Soltero, Xue-Ying LiYang, Laurent Molino, Ryan Plumadore, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Vladimir Falko, Adina Luican-Mayer
abstract: Controlling the stacking and rotational registry of graphene layers provides a powerful handle on atomic-scale structural reconstructions that alter the electronic landscape at the nanoscale. In particular, this governs how massless and massive Dirac fermions coexist and interact at the monolayer-bilayer graphene interface. In the limit of marginal twist, the system reconstructs into domains of distinct vertical stacking order, introducing characteristic electronic properties and new electronic length scales, a regime that, despite its structural richness, remains largely unexplored. Here, using scanning tunnelling microscopy and spectroscopy, we demonstrate that at very low rotation angles the monolayer-bilayer graphene system relaxes into a network of three distinct stacking domains with individual electronic textures revealed through spatially resolved spectroscopic mapping and corroborated by computed local density of states. We report switching of the hierarchy of the tunnelling characteristics between Bernal and rhombohedral domains as a function of bias voltage. Furthermore, the measured spectroscopic maps exhibit theoretically anticipated domain wall 'twirling' around energetically unfavorable AAB stacking nodes, promoted by out-of-plane deformations. Our results shed light on fundamental structure-property relationships underpinning moiré-driven phenomena, opening new avenues for harnessing structural degrees of freedom in van der Waals heterostructures.
summary: This paper investigates how atomic-scale reconstruction in nearly zero-degree twisted graphene creates a network of distinct stacking domains. By using STM/STS, the authors show that these domains have unique electronic signatures and that structural deformations drive complex domain wall patterns. This work provides fundamental insights into how structural degrees of freedom can be used to engineer electronic properties in 2D materials.
main_problem: Exploring the electronic properties and structural textures of the 'marginally twisted' regime in monolayer-bilayer graphene, where structural reconstruction into distinct stacking domains dominates over a uniform moiré potential.
main_result: The study identifies three distinct stacking domains (ABC, ABA, and AAB) and demonstrates that domain wall 'twirling' is driven by out-of-plane deformations, with electronic features switching as a function of bias voltage.
method: The researchers used Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM) and Spectroscopy (STS) for experimental characterization, combined with a hybrid k.p tight-binding model and mesoscale lattice relaxation calculations using the L-BFGS algorithm.
model_or_system: A marginally twisted monolayer-bilayer graphene heterostructure on hBN at a very low rotation angle (approx. 0.3 degrees), featuring a reconstructed network of ABC, ABA, and AAB stacking domains.
key_observables: Local Density of States (LDOS), dI/dV maps, STM topography (apparent height), spectroscopic line maps, and strain tensors (hydrostatic and shear).
important_parameters: Twist angle theta approx 0.3 degrees, moire superlattice period lambda approx 47 nm, domain wall width approx 7-8 nm, and maximum pseudomagnetic field approx 20 T.
main_assumptions: The model assumes the interface between the first two layers is Bernal stacked and that spatial variations in the local twist angle are due to long-wavelength extrinsic strain.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the STM setup and stacking schematics; Figure 2 presents STS spectra and calculated band dispersions/DOS; Figure 3 illustrates 3D topography and domain wall twirling; Figure 4 shows topographic maps at different bias voltages.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of structural reconstruction, describes the experimental and theoretical methods, presents the identification of stacking domains and their electronic signatures, analyzes domain wall dynamics and structural deformations, and concludes with the implications for van der Waals heterostructures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
